message.content:
Independent verifier for GitHub issue #24 in /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch main). You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. Run repros with /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git, no gh.

FINDING (#24, stated LOW): Firewall A requires an external-claim hypothesis's predicted direction to come from the claim, not the blind baseline. Hypothesis enforces source_claim_id for EXTERNAL_CLAIM, but predicted_direction (whole-corpus) / direction_supports_claim (unit) are free parameters chosen at confirm time, unconnected to ClaimRecord.assertion — so a direction can be picked after seeing baseline results (post-hoc direction / HARKing variant).

Verify by reading hypothesis.py, claims.py, confirm.py. Consider counter-arguments: is this a design-completeness/roadmap gap rather than a coded contradiction? Is binding direction at lock time the right fix? Is LOW appropriate? Decide.

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high|medium|low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by reading the relevant files. Let me start.

content.content:
1	"""Typed, falsifiable hypotheses (METHODOLOGY.md §3).
2	
3	Every hypothesis names what is claimed, the test, the data it runs on, and a pre-specified
4	decision rule. Two origins:
5	
6	- ``DISCOVERY`` — surfaced by the data, then made specific, locked, and timestamped before
7	  confirmation (Firewall B).
8	- ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` — derived by the adapter from an external expert claim, tested against
9	  the blind baseline (Firewall A).
10	
11	Two kinds, which select the confirmation mechanism:
12	
13	- ``UNIT_LEVEL`` — confirmed on a held-out split.
14	- ``WHOLE_CORPUS`` — no held-out object possible; confirmed against null/permutation
15	  distributions and stability across resamples.
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
21	from enum import Enum
22	from typing import Any, Mapping
23	
24	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
25	
26	
27	class HypothesisKind(Enum):
28	    UNIT_LEVEL = "unit_level"
29	    WHOLE_CORPUS = "whole_corpus"
30	
31	
32	class HypothesisOrigin(Enum):
33	    DISCOVERY = "discovery"
34	    EXTERNAL_CLAIM = "external_claim"
35	
36	
37	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
38	class Hypothesis:
39	    """A locked, falsifiable assertion ready for confirmatory testing.
40	
41	    ``locked_at`` and ``timestamp_proof`` are populated when the hypothesis is sealed for
42	    pre-registration. An unlocked hypothesis (either field ``None``) must not be passed to a
43	    confirmatory test — the engine refuses this, and the ``locked`` property is the in-code
44	    check.
45	
46	    Scope of ``locked`` today (audit pass 1, issue #6): ``locked`` is a **pre-registration
47	    sentinel** — it asserts only that both fields are populated. It does NOT yet parse
48	    ``locked_at`` as a timestamp, validate ``timestamp_proof`` as an RFC-3161 token, or
49	    verify that the lock precedes confirmation. Real RFC-3161 verification and
50	    lock-before-confirm ordering are deferred until the pre-registration / timestamp-authority
51	    infrastructure lands (see GOVERNANCE.md). Do not read ``locked`` as a cryptographic
52	    guarantee yet.
53	    """
54	
55	    hypothesis_id: str
56	    statement: str
57	    kind: HypothesisKind
58	    origin: HypothesisOrigin
59	    test_name: str
60	    decision_rule: str
61	    source_claim_id: str | None = None
62	    locked_at: str | None = None
63	    timestamp_proof: str | None = None
64	    params: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
65	
66	    @property
67	    def locked(self) -> bool:
68	        return self.locked_at is not None and self.timestamp_proof is not None
69	
70	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
71	        if self.origin is HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM and not self.source_claim_id:
72	            raise ValueError("EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypotheses must carry a source_claim_id")
73	        object.__setattr__(self, "params", freeze_mapping(self.params))
74

1	"""External-claims source interface — optional, and quarantined from the baseline.
2	
3	If a dataset ships claims asserted by some external authority (relationships, labels, a
4	taxonomy, mappings), an adapter exposes them through :class:`ExternalClaimsSource`. The
5	engine converts each :class:`ClaimRecord` into a typed, falsifiable hypothesis and tests it
6	against the baseline — which is kept blind to these claims (Firewall A).
7	
8	This interface is *structurally separable*: the baseline pipeline never receives it, so the
9	baseline can be produced with claims withheld. Pure-discovery datasets omit it entirely.
10	Access to a claims source while claim-blindness is in force raises ``FirewallViolation``
11	(see :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.firewalls`).
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
17	from typing import Any, Iterable, Mapping, Protocol
18	
19	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
20	
21	
22	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
23	class ClaimRecord:
24	    """One external, expert-asserted claim about the data — a falsifiable assertion.
25	
26	    The engine treats this as a *hypothesis source*, never as ground truth. ``subject`` and
27	    ``referents`` name the units the claim is about; ``assertion`` describes what is claimed
28	    in a typed, machine-readable way; ``provenance`` records who asserted it and where from.
29	    """
30	
31	    claim_id: str
32	    subject: str
33	    referents: tuple[str, ...]
34	    assertion: Mapping[str, Any]
35	    provenance: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
36	
37	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
38	        object.__setattr__(self, "assertion", freeze_mapping(self.assertion))
39	        object.__setattr__(self, "provenance", freeze_mapping(self.provenance))
40	
41	
42	class ExternalClaimsSource(Protocol):
43	    """Adapter-provided access to a dataset's external claims (adjudication mode only)."""
44	
45	    def claims(self) -> Iterable[ClaimRecord]:
46	        """Yield every external claim to be adjudicated against the blind baseline."""
47	        ...
48	
49	    def claim_fingerprint(self) -> str:
50	        """Stable content hash of the claim set, for provenance and pre-registration."""
51	        ...
52

1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import math
27	from typing import Iterable, Literal
28	
29	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
30	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
31	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
32	
33	Direction = Literal["greater", "less"]
34	
35	
36	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
37	    """Gate: a hypothesis must be locked + timestamped before any confirmatory step."""
38	    if not hypothesis.locked:
39	        raise FirewallViolation(
40	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
41	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
42	        )
43	
44	
45	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
46	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
47	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
48	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
49	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
50	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
51	
52	
53	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
54	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
55	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
56	
57	
58	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
59	    if not math.isfinite(value):
60	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
61	
62	
63	def verdict_from_pvalue(
64	    hypothesis_id: str,
65	    *,
66	    statistic: float,
67	    p_value: float,
68	    alpha: float,
69	    powered: bool,
70	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
71	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
72	) -> Verdict:
73	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
74	
75	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
76	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
77	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
78	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
79	
80	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
81	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the
82	    recorded decision rule reflects the caller's ``test_description`` rather than a hard-coded
83	    (and previously incorrect) sidedness label (issue #2).
84	    """
85	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
86	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
87	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
88	
89	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
90	    common = dict(statistic=statistic, threshold=alpha, evidence={"p_value": p_value})
91	    if not powered:
92	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
93	    if p_value <= alpha:
94	        if direction_supports_claim:
95	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
96	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
97	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
98	        hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common
99	    )
100	
101	
102	def confirm_unit_level(
103	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
104	    *,
105	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
106	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
107	    statistic: float,
108	    p_value: float,
109	    alpha: float,
110	    powered: bool,
111	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
112	) -> Verdict:
113	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only."""
114	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
115	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
116	    require_locked(hypothesis)
117	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
118	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
119	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
120	        statistic=statistic,
121	        p_value=p_value,
122	        alpha=alpha,
123	        powered=powered,
124	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
125	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
126	    )
127	
128	
129	def _empirical_p(null: list[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
130	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
131	    n = len(null)
132	    if tail == "greater":
133	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
134	    else:
135	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
136	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
137	
138	
139	def confirm_whole_corpus(
140	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
141	    *,
142	    observed: float,
143	    null_distribution: list[float],
144	    alpha: float,
145	    stable_across_resamples: bool,
146	    predicted_direction: Direction,
147	) -> Verdict:
148	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + stability check.
149	
150	    ``predicted_direction`` is the tail the claim predicts the observed statistic lies in
151	    (``"greater"`` or ``"less"`` than the null). The pattern must (a) beat the null at
152	    ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and (b) be stable across resamples → ``supported``. A
153	    result significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``. Anything else (unstable,
154	    or not significant in either tail) → ``indeterminate``.
155	    """
156	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
157	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
158	    require_locked(hypothesis)
159	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
160	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
161	    if not null_distribution:
162	        raise ValueError("a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation")
163	    for v in null_distribution:
164	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
165	
166	    opposite: Direction = "less" if predicted_direction == "greater" else "greater"
167	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, predicted_direction)
168	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
169	    n = len(null_distribution)
170	    rule = (
171	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail "
172	        f"'{predicted_direction}'; requires stability across resamples"
173	    )
174	    evidence = {
175	        "p_support": p_support,
176	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
177	        "null_n": n,
178	        "stable": stable_across_resamples,
179	        "predicted_direction": predicted_direction,
180	    }
181	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
182	
183	    if not stable_across_resamples:
184	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
185	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
186	        )
187	    if p_support <= alpha:
188	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
189	    if p_contra <= alpha:
190	        return Verdict.contradicted(
191	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
192	            rule,
193	            notes="significant in the opposite tail",
194	            **common,
195	        )
196	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
197	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
198	    )
199

content.text: The finding is confirmed at the code level. Let me verify the counter-arguments by checking how `assertion` is structured and whether any adapter/binding exists that connects claim assertion to predicted direction.

content.text: The grep confirms there is no code path that derives `predicted_direction` or `direction_supports_claim` from `ClaimRecord.assertion`. The `assertion` field is only ever frozen, never read. Let me check whether an adapter layer exists that's supposed to do this conversion, and check the firewalls module for context.

input.command: cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && grep -rln "ClaimRecord\|from_claim\|to_hypothesis\|adapter\|EXTERNAL_CLAIM\|HypothesisOrigin" --include="*.py" src/ tests/ | grep -v __pycache__; echo "---FIREWALL DOC---"; grep -rn "Firewall A" --include="*.py" --include="*.md" /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/docs 2>/dev/null | grep -v __pycache__ | head -20

content.content:
documented.

**Firewall A — claim-blindness (adjudication mode only).** When external claims exist, the
baseline must be built **blind to those claims.** The external source's answers — its
asserted relationships, strengths, labels, taxonomy — must never enter baseline
construction as inputs, features, or training labels. If they do, "the baseline agrees
with the claims" is circular and the result is void. The baseline is built from the data;
the claims are quarantined until adjudication.

**Firewall B — discover / confirm separation.** A pattern used to *discover* a hypothesis
must not be the data used to *confirm* it. Discovering a pattern and then "testing" it on
the same data proves nothing. So:

- discover on an exploration partition (or via descriptive analysis), lock the now-specific
  claim, then
- confirm on data the discovery never touched.

For unit-level claims this is a **held-out split**. For whole-corpus claims (where you
cannot hold out part of the object — e.g. global structure or coverage), confirmation is
against **null / permutation distributions** and **stability across resamples**: the
pattern must beat chance on something it was not fit to. Same principle, different
mechanism.

## 3. Hypotheses

Every hypothesis is **typed and falsifiable** — it names what is claimed, the test, the
data it runs on, and a pre-specified decision rule.

- **Discovery mode:** the data surfaces candidate hypotheses; they are made specific,
  **locked, and timestamped before confirmation** (see Firewall B). The questions are
  data-derived, which is legitimate *because* discovery and confirmation use different
  data — not because the questions were guessed in advance.
- **Adjudicate-external-claims mode:** each external claim is itself a pre-stated,
  falsifiable assertion about the data. The adapter converts each into a typed hypothesis.
  These are tested against the blind baseline.

"The claim" and "whether the claim is correct" are kept as separate objects throughout.

## 4. Verdicts

Confirmatory tests return one of **three** verdicts — never a forced binary:

- **Supported** — the baseline corroborates the claim/hypothesis at the pre-specified
  threshold.
- **Contradicted** — the baseline is inconsistent with the claim at the pre-specified
  threshold.
- **Indeterminate** — the method cannot decide: underpowered, out of the measurement's
  reach, or the disagreement is plausibly a limitation of the method rather than of the
===ADR-0005===
# ADR-0005 — Two firewalls: claim-blindness and discover/confirm separation

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-15)

## Context

Two distinct ways to fool yourself: (1) build the reference using the very claims you mean
to test (circularity), and (2) confirm a pattern on the same data you discovered it on
(double-dipping). Both must be prevented structurally, not by good intentions.

## Decision

**Firewall A — claim-blindness.** When external claims exist, the baseline is built blind to
them: the claim source's answers never enter baseline construction as inputs, features, or
labels. Enforced by making the external-claims source structurally separable from the
baseline pipeline, so the baseline can be produced with it withheld.

**Firewall B — discover/confirm separation.** The data used to discover a hypothesis is not
the data used to confirm it. Unit-level claims: a held-out split sealed until pre-
registration. Whole-corpus claims (no held-out object possible): null/permutation
distributions plus stability across resamples. Same principle, different mechanism.

## Consequences

- Data-derived questions are legitimate, because discovery and confirmation use different
  data — this is what separates the method from HARKing.
- The engine must provide both confirmation mechanisms and refuse to confirm on
  discovery data.
- Both firewalls are enforced in code and exercised by tests, not asserted in docstrings.

content.text:
I have enough evidence. The key methodological principle is explicit: METHODOLOGY.md §3 says "each external claim is itself a pre-stated, falsifiable assertion... The adapter converts each into a typed hypothesis." The claim's `assertion` is supposed to carry the predicted direction, and the conversion is supposed to bind it. The code does not do this.

Let me verify one final counter-argument: whether the `assertion` field is even structured to carry a direction (so the gap is real, not just "claims don't have directions").

The `ClaimRecord.assertion` is a free-form `Mapping[str, Any]` with no schema — so there is no typed direction to bind even if someone wrote the adapter. This actually strengthens the "design-completeness gap" reading.

## Verdict

VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE
CONFIDENCE: high

SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: LOW is appropriate; arguably borderline LOW/INFORMATIONAL. The gap is real but it is a missing-component gap, not an active contradiction or a defeated guard. No claim-to-hypothesis adapter exists yet, so there is no shipped code path that produces a post-hoc direction in practice — the integrity hole only becomes exploitable once the (currently absent) EXTERNAL_CLAIM pipeline is built. The methodology's own framing ("the adapter converts each claim into a typed hypothesis") confirms the binding is part of the intended design, not yet realized. Keep at LOW.

REASONING (own evidence):
- `hypothesis.py:70-72` — `__post_init__` enforces only that an `EXTERNAL_CLAIM` hypothesis carries a non-empty `source_claim_id`. Nothing ties the rest of the hypothesis to the referenced claim's content.
- `hypothesis.py` has no `predicted_direction` field at all; for whole-corpus it is supplied at confirm time as a free parameter (`confirm.py:146 predicted_direction: Direction`), and for unit-level the analogous `direction_supports_claim` is a free boolean (`confirm.py:70,111`). The confirm docstring (`confirm.py:80-83`) explicitly says these "must be derived by the caller" — i.e. the binding is delegated to a caller, with no enforcement.
- `claims.py` — `ClaimRecord.assertion` is read nowhere in `src/`. A whole-repo grep for `.assertion` returns only the line that freezes it (`claims.py:38`). So the claim's asserted content never flows into either direction parameter.
- There is no claim→hypothesis adapter in the codebase: `grep "Hypothesis("` matches only `tests/test_confirm.py:22,35`. The "adapter converts each ClaimRecord into a typed hypothesis" described in METHODOLOGY.md §3 / claims.py:5-6 is not implemented. Hence direction is, today, only ever chosen by hand at confirm time.
- Refutation attempts that failed: (a) No schema or validator constrains `assertion` to encode a direction, so the claim cannot currently bind direction even in principle — confirms the gap rather than refuting it. (b) Firewall B (`require_locked` + lock-before-confirm) does not cover this: per hypothesis.py:46-52, `locked` is only a "pre-registration sentinel" (both fields populated), it does NOT verify lock precedes confirmation, and even a real lock would not help because `predicted_direction` is passed to `confirm_whole_corpus` separately and is not part of the locked `Hypothesis` object — so locking the hypothesis does not lock the direction. This is the crux: the finding is correct that direction lives outside the pre-registered object.
- ADR-0005 grounds HARKing-avoidance in Firewall B for discovery mode; for EXTERNAL_CLAIM mode the analogous protection is that the claim is "pre-stated." If the predicted direction is not carried from the pre-stated claim into the locked hypothesis, that pre-statement guarantee is not realized in code — exactly the finding's "post-hoc direction / HARKing variant."

So this is a genuine design-completeness gap that is simultaneously a real (if currently dormant) integrity hole. Calling it merely "roadmap" understates it; calling it a live exploitable contradiction overstates it. PARTIAL was considered, but the finding's core technical assertions are all literally true and verifiable in code, so TRUE_POSITIVE is correct, with the severity nuance captured above.

FIX_GUIDANCE:
- Bind direction at lock time, yes — that is the right fix. Add a pre-registered direction to the `Hypothesis` object itself (e.g. `predicted_direction: Direction | None` and/or `direction_supports_claim` semantics) so it is part of the frozen, locked, timestamped artifact, not a confirm-time argument.
- Have `confirm_whole_corpus` / `confirm_unit_level` read direction from the locked hypothesis instead of accepting it as a free parameter (or, transitionally, assert the passed value equals the locked one).
- Give `ClaimRecord.assertion` enough typed structure to carry a direction (or a derivable predicate), and implement the claim→hypothesis adapter that METHODOLOGY.md §3 already promises, so the direction provably originates in the claim.
- Lower-effort interim: a `__post_init__`/adapter check that an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis's direction field is populated from its source claim, even before full RFC-3161 lock-ordering lands. Track alongside the existing pre-registration deferral noted in hypothesis.py:46-52 and GOVERNANCE.md.
